evidence.claim: The announced Arizona plan is a fabrication buildout, so the project creates chip output rather than depending on allocated chips as a primary input.
The public funding terms describe fab construction and production milestones, not a scarce external chip allocation needed to complete the site.
TSMC reports capacity expansion as a capital and manufacturing execution program, which makes chip allocation a lower-probability binding factor for this plan.
The Department of Energy treats large power transformers as a grid resilience and supply-chain risk area.
DOE's transformer supply-chain report identifies long lead times and constrained domestic capacity for transformer equipment.
Local utility grid improvement work is material to large Phoenix-area load additions, so transformer timing remains a medium-risk factor.
The Phoenix Active Management Area is a regulated groundwater area, making long-horizon water availability a direct project constraint.
Phoenix publishes water-resource planning material for drought and growth management, which keeps water supply visible in any large industrial plan.
TSMC Arizona describes environmental and water-management commitments, indicating that water remains an explicitly managed input for the site.
TSMC publicly linked the Arizona schedule to a shortage of skilled workers for equipment installation.
BLS occupational data for the Phoenix metro area bounds the local pool for construction and technical labor needed by a fab buildout.
Arizona's semiconductor-development material emphasizes workforce and supplier-base development as part of the sector buildout.
City planning and development review remains a required path for large site work in Phoenix.
Maricopa County air-quality permitting is relevant to major industrial construction and operations.
Arizona environmental permits can affect industrial project sequencing, though public evidence makes this less binding than labor for this plan.
